calendar.matters:
Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Mandatory Fee Arbitration And Stay Proceedings
Off Calendar Mar-20-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT VIKRANT PUNJ's Motion To Compel Mandatory Fee Arbitration And Stay Proceedings.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant Vikrant Kumar Punj's motion to compel mandatory fee arbitration and stay proceedings is denied because the demand was not timely made.

"The [Mandatory Fee Arbitration Act (MFAA), Business & Professions Code, Secs. 6200 et seq.,] establishes a system of arbitration before local bar associations in attorney fee disputes to provide an effective inexpensive remedy to a client which does not necessitate the hiring of a second attorney. Under the MFAA, arbitration is optional for the client, but mandatory for attorneys if properly initiated by the client. Attorneys are required to notify any client disputing legal fees or costs of the client's right to arbitration by sending a written notice prior to, or at, the time the lawyer begins any collection related proceeding against the client. (Bus. & Prof. Code, Sec. 6201, subd. (a).) '[T]he client's failure to request arbitration within 30 days after receipt of notice from the attorney shall be deemed a waiver of the client's right to arbitration . . . .' (Ibid.)" (Levinson Arshonsky & Kurtz LLP v. Kim (2019) 35 Cal.App.5th 896, 900 [some quotation marks, footnote, and some citations omitted].)

Here, plaintiff Dharma Law P.C. served written notice of the client's arbitration option on defendant Punj on March 12, 2024 (Bhandari Decl., para. 19 & Ex. B), and on October 8, 2025 with service of the complaint (id., para. 24, Exs. E & F). Punj answered the complaint on November 11, 2025, and first demanded arbitration with the filing of this motion on February 20, 2026. "[T]he client's failure to request arbitration within 30 days after receipt of notice from the attorney shall be deemed a waiver of the client's right to arbitration under the provisions of this article." (Bus. & Prof. Code, Sec. 6201, subd. (a).) "The request for arbitration shall be served and filed prior to the filing of an answer in the action or equivalent response in the other proceeding; failure to so request arbitration prior to the filing of an answer or equivalent response shall be deemed a waiver of the client's right to arbitration under the provisions of this article . . . ." (Id., Sec. 6201, subd. (b).) These statutory provisions are enforced according to their plain terms.

Because Punj's arbitration demand came after his answer and more than 30 days after his receipt of notice of his arbitration option, the demand was waived. This motion is denied.

The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed form of order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reclassification From Limited Civil Case To Unlimited Civil Case; Declaration Of Counsel Naryan P. Bhandari
Off Calendar Mar-20-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PLAINTIFF DHARMA LAW P.C.'S, A CALIFORNIA PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION, Motion For Reclassification From Limited Civil Case To Unlimited Civil Case.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff Dharma Law P.C.'s unopposed motion to reclassify this case as an unlimited civil case is denied.

Dharma Law filed the case as a breach of contract action and prayed for damages of $17,162.38, plus attorneys' fees. Based on the prayer, the case is a limited civil case. (See Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 85, subd. (a) [case is a limited civil case if "[t]he amount in controversy does not exceed thirty-five thousand dollars ($35,000). As used in this section, 'amount in controversy' means the amount of the demand, or the recovery sought, . . ., that is in controversy in the action, exclusive of attorneys' fees, interest, and costs."]; id., Sec. 86, subd. (a)(1) [civil case is limited civil case if it is "[a] case at law if the demand, exclusive of interest, or the value of the property in controversy amounts to thirty-five thousand dollars ($35,000) or less. . . ."].)

Defendant Vikrant Kumar Punj's answer raises affirmative defenses that Dharma Law acted negligently and in bad faith, and coerced Punj. But Punj did not file a cross-complaint. Nothing in Punj's answer demands affirmative relief; affirmative relief must be sought by cross-complaint and may not be granted based on an answer. (See Construction Protective Services, Inc. v. TIG Specialty Ins. Co. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 189, 198 ["If, in addition, the defendant seeks affirmative relief in its favor (such as the recovery of damages), it must file a cross-complaint, because section 431.30, subdivision (c), bars it from claiming affirmative relief by way of the answer."].)

Dharma Law separately raises the contention that the quantum meruit value of its performance is greater than the contract amount. This contention is irrelevant in light of its pleading: it is demanding relief only under the contract and has not asserted a quasi-contract theory of recovery.

Finally, Dharma Law's demand for fees under the contract does not alter the analysis because fees are not included in the amount in controversy for purposes of classification. (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 85, subd. (a).)

This order is without prejudice to a renewed request in the event the pleadings are amended.

The prevailing party is required to prepare and submit to Dept. 301 a proposed form of order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Christine Van Aken. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (301/CVA)
